tate of Califor'1ia 
air Political Practices Commissio1l, 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Samuel Gorlick. 
City Attorney 
City of Burbank 
P.O. Box 6459 
Burbank, CA 
91510 
Dear Mr. Gorlick: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/Legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
November 30, 1981 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for the additional information you provided 
in your letter dated October 6, 1981. 
I apologize for any 
inconvenience caused by the delay in my response. 
Since 
there are no specific decisions now pending for which Mrs. Howard 
needs advice, I can only give you general guidance in determining 
whether or not Mrs. Howard's disqualification will be required 
as to future City Council decisions; however, if you would 
like further advice as to a particular decision or decisions, 
we will be happy to provide you wi,h advice pursuant to 
Government Code Section 831l4(b).-
My understanding of the situation from your letters is 
as follows. 
Mary Lou Howard is a member of the Burbank city 
Couflcil. 
She and her husband own an undivided one-half 
interest in real property located at 272 East Angeleno Avenue 
in Burbank. 
This property is included within the City Center 
Redevelopment Project Area; the City Council serves as the 
Redevelopment Agency. 
The Council approved an agreement 
with a developer to redevelop a two-block area which included 
the Howards' property. 
Thereafter, a summons and complaint 
in eminent domain.was filed by the Council to acquire the 
Howards' property. 
Condemnation proceedings were 
so filed 
with respect to all other properties within the two-block 
area. 
In the pending litigation, the property owners are 
contesting the Council's right to condemn. 
Mrs. Howard has 
not participated in the decisions pertaining to the condemnation 
of her property. 
All statutory references are to the Government 
Code. 


Samuel Gorlick 
November 30, 1981 
Page Two 
You asked to what extent Mrs. Howard may participate in 
other Council decisions concerning the Redevelopment Area 
that do not directly relate to her property. 
Such decisions 
include resolutions approving disposition and development 
agreements and resolutions of necessity for land acquisition 
proceedings or resolutions setting hearings to consider such 
matters. 
As you know, the Political Reform Act states: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
Section 87100. 
An official has a financial interest in a governmental 
decision when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect on any real property 
in which the official has an interest worth more than $1,000. 
Section 87103. 
Under these sections, several elements must be present 
before Mrs. Howard would be required to disqualify herself 
from participation in a decision concerning the Redevelopment 
Area. 
First, it must be reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a financial effect. 
Second, the anticipated 
financial effect must be on Mrs. Howard's interest in real 
property. 
Third, the anticipated effect must be material, 
and, fourth, the effect must be distinguishable from its 
effect on the public generally. 
As you indicated in your letter, Mrs. Howard must clearly 
refrain from part~cipating in Council decisions which directly 
pertain to her property. 
These would include decisions on 
the pending eminent domain proceedings and other decisions 
that could affect the outcome of the litigation. 
Any other Council decisions which could affect the value 
of Mrs. Howard's real property interest would also potentially 
require disqualification. 
However, since Mrs. Howard's 
property is the subject of condemnation proceedings, the 
value of the real property may not be subject to normal 
market forces. 
Accordingly, whether di 
ualification would 
be required depends on (I) whether she is contesting the 


Samuel Gorlick 
November 30, 1981 
Page Three 
proceedings, and (2) when the value of the property is set 
for compensation purposes. 
If Mrs. Howard does not contest 
the condemnation of her property and the value of the property 
for purposes of compensation has been set at the time of 
filing of the complaint in eminent domain and cannot be 
changed by subsequent events, future actions of the Council 
could not have a "material financial effect" on Mrs. Howard's 
real property within the meaning of Section 87103 which 
would require disqualification. 
Conversely, if she does 
contest the eminent domain proceeding or if events subsequent 
to the filing of the action can influence the value of the 
property for purposes of compensation in the proceedings, 
there is the potential for Council decisions which could 
affect the property. 
Whether disqualification would be required as to a 
particular decision would depend on a factual analysis of 
the foreseeable effects of the individual decision on the 
value of Mrs. Howard's real property. 
Relevant factors 
would include the proximity of Mrs. Howard's property to the 
subject matter of the decision, and the magnitude of the 
anticipated impact of the decision on her property. 
I have enclosed copies of several Commission Opinions 
and Commission Regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, 
which will assist you in analyzing potential conflict situations 
as they develop. 
If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to 
contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
DMF:plh 
Enclosures: 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 
1 FPPC Opinions 71 
2 FPPC Opinions 77 
3 FPPC Opinions 38 
4 FPPC Opinions 19 


October 6, 1981 
Ms. Diane Maura Fisburn, Counsel 
Legal Division, F.P.P.C. 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Ms. Fishburn: 
\ 
Thank you for your letter dated September 28, 1981. 
The answer to your first question is "yes." 
Mrs. Howard still owns an individed interest in the property 
being acquired for redevelopment purposes. 
The status of the eminent domain proceeding is that 
the owners have filed their answers, and the Agency is 
in the process of obtaining a date for the First Pretrial 
Conference. 
The answer of the defendants contests the 
agency's right to condemn. 
The Agency has deposited the 
probable compensation into Court. 
There are no specific decisions now pending before 
the City Council for which the advice is needed. 
Rather, 
the advice is needed in order that Mrs. Howard may know 
whether or not she may vote on routine matters concerning 
other Agency business within the project area where her 
property is located. 
Such matters include resolutions 
approving disposition and development agreements and 
resolutions of necessity for land acquisition proceedings 
or resolutions setting hearings to consider such matters. 
Your early response will be greatly appreciated. 
Very truly yours, 
SAMUEL 
Ci ty Attorney 
RWM:SG:ad 
$ 
0 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
Chief Legal Division 
FPPC 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
Last year, I discussed the following situation 
with Mr. Dickerson (who is no longer with your office). 
A member of the Burbank City Council (Mary Lou 
Howard) and her husband own an undivided one-half 
interest in property at 272 East Angeleno Avenue in the 
City of Burbank. 
This property is in the City Center 
Redevelopment Project Area. 
The City Council is also 
the Redevelopment Agency. 
The City Council and Agency 
had before it an agreement with a developer to redevelop 
a two block area which included the Howard property. 
It appeared that Mrs. Howard's participation would be 
needed to act on the matter. 
Mr. Dickerson advised me verbally after discussing 
the situation with staff, that Mrs. Howard could be qualified 
to act free of any conflict of interest if prior thereto the 
Agency authorized condemnation of her property with Mrs. 
Howard not participating, voting, or influencing such 
action. 
Filing of the summons and complaint (which fixes 
the date of valuation) would remove any possible financial 
interest in the proposed agreement to develop her property. 
Written confirmation of this advice was thereafter rendered 
unnecessary when a recalcitrant member of the City Council 
changed 
s mind and Mrs. Howard's vote was no longer 
needed. 
The project was then approved without Mrs. Howard's 
vote. 
Thereafter, a summons and complaint in eminent domain 
was filed to acquire the Howard property. 
Condemnation 
proceedings were also 
led with respect to all other 
s 
two bl 
area of 
project. 
& 
0 


FPPC 
-2-
August 31, 1981 
Although the need for a written opinion was 
rendered unnecessary Mrs. Howard would now like to have such 
opinion before 
acts on other proposed develoome!n 
within the City Centre Redevelopment Project area. 
The 
Howards do not have an interest in any other property 
within said area. 
A reply at your earliest convenience will be 
greatly appreciated. 
SG:keb 
Sincerely, 
SAf\1UEL GLICK 
City Attorney

sections.question: To what extent may Mrs. Howard participate in other Council decisions concerning the Redevelopment Area that do not directly relate to her property, such as resolutions approving disposition and development agreements and resolutions of necessity for land acquisition proceedings?
sections.conclusion: Mrs. Howard must clearly refrain from participating in Council decisions which directly pertain to her property and the pending eminent domain proceedings. Whether disqualification would be required for other Council decisions affecting the value of her real property depends on factual analysis: if she does not contest the condemnation and property value is fixed at filing of the complaint, future actions could not have a material financial effect; conversely, if she contests the proceedings or subsequent events can influence property value, disqualification may be required based on whether the foreseeable effects of individual decisions would have a material impact on her property.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether City Councilmember Mary Lou Howard must disqualify herself from voting on redevelopment decisions within the City Center Redevelopment Project Area where her property is subject to condemnation proceedings, particularly regarding decisions that do not directly pertain to her specific property.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mrs. Howard must disqualify herself from decisions directly concerning her property and the condemnation proceedings. For other redevelopment decisions, disqualification is only required if Council action would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property value, which depends on whether she contests the condemnation and whether the property value can be affected by subsequent Council actions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters in reverse chronological order (Nov 30 FPPC response, Oct 6 follow-up, Aug 31 initial request). Primary FPPC advice letter dated November 30, 1981. Clear question and conclusion sections present in main response letter. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not significantly impact extraction.

QUESTION: To what extent may Mrs. Howard participate in other Council decisions concerning the Redevelopment Area that do not directly relate to her property, such as resolutions approving disposition and development agreements and resolutions of necessity for land acquisition proceedings?

CONCLUSION: Mrs. Howard must clearly refrain from participating in Council decisions which directly pertain to her property and the pending eminent domain proceedings. Whether disqualification would be required for other Council decisions affecting the value of her real property depends on factual analysis: if she does not contest the condemnation and property value is fixed at filing of the complaint, future actions could not have a material financial effect; conversely, if she contests the proceedings or subsequent events can influence property value, disqualification may be required based on whether the foreseeable effects of individual decisions would have a material impact on her property.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Diane Maura Fisburn advises City Attorney Samuel Gorlick that Councilmember Mary Lou Howard's disqualification requirement from redevelopment decisions depends on whether Council actions would have a material financial effect on her property interest that is distinguishable from effects on the public generally, with specific application to her condemned property in the redevelopment area.
